title: JavaScript Destructuring
tldr: Destructuring is a JavaScript expression that unpacks values from arrays or properties from objects into distinct variables.
Array destructuring: const [a, b] = [1, 2] assigns a=1, b=2. Supports skipping ([, , c]), defaults, and rest.
Object destructuring: const { name, age } = obj assigns name=obj.name, age=obj.age. Supports renaming ({ name: userName }) and defaults.
Destructuring works in variable declarations, function parameters, assignments, and loops.
laymanDefinition: Imagine you receive a gift box that says 'Mixed Fruits' on it. Inside are apples, bananas, and oranges. Instead of unpacking them one by one saying 'this is an apple, this is a banana,' destructuring lets you say 'I want the apple and the banana' and the JavaScript engine automatically extracts them for you. It's like having a machine that opens boxes and hands you exactly the items you asked for, in the order you specified.

deepDive.heading: Array Destructuring — Positional Unpacking
deepDive.text: Array destructuring uses position to match variables to values: const [a, b, c] = [1, 2, 3]. You can skip elements with commas: const [, , third] = arr. Default values protect against undefined: const [a = 5, b = 10] = [1] gives a=1, b=10. Swapping variables: [a, b] = [b, a] is a classic use.

deepDive.heading: Object Destructuring — Named Unpacking
deepDive.text: Object destructuring uses property names to match: const { name, age } = person. Rename with colon: const { name: personName, age: personAge } = person. Defaults with renaming: const { name = 'Guest', role = 'user' } = person. Nested destructuring: const { address: { city } } = person.

deepDive.heading: Destructuring in Function Parameters
deepDive.text: Destructuring can extract specific properties from an object/array passed as an argument: function greet({ name, age }) { ... }. This makes function signatures self-documenting. Combine with defaults: function connect({ host = 'localhost', port = 3000 } = {}). This is the standard pattern for options objects.

deepDive.heading: Nested Destructuring and Complex Patterns
deepDive.text: Destructuring works at any depth: const { data: { user: { name } } } = response. Mix array and object destructuring: const { users: [first, ...rest] } = data. Use with the rest operator: const [head, ...tail] = arr or const { name, ...details } = obj. These patterns are common in React hooks and API responses.

deepDive.heading: Destructuring Use Cases and Best Practices
list: API responses: Extract specific fields from JSON responses.
Function options: Cleanly extract configuration from an options object.
React hooks: const [state, setState] = useState(initial) and const { data, loading } = useQuery().
Swapping variables: [a, b] = [b, a] — no temp variable needed.

interviewAnswer: Destructuring is a JavaScript syntax that unpacks values from arrays (by position) or properties from objects (by name) into variables. Array destructuring uses square brackets: const [a, b] = [1, 2]. Supports skipping, defaults, and rest (...). Object destructuring uses curly braces: const { name, age } = obj. Supports renaming (colon), defaults, nested destructuring, and rest. Destructuring is commonly used in function parameters to extract options objects, in React for useState/useEffect, in imports, and for variable swapping. It makes code more readable by reducing repetitive property access.

interviewQuestions.question: What is destructuring in JavaScript?
interviewQuestions.answer: Destructuring is a syntax that unpacks values from arrays or objects into distinct variables. Array destructuring uses positions: const [a, b] = [1, 2]. Object destructuring uses property names: const { name } = { name: 'Alice' }.

interviewQuestions.question: How do you skip elements in array destructuring?
interviewQuestions.answer: Use commas without variable names: const [, , third] = [1, 2, 3]. The first two elements are skipped, third gets 3.

interviewQuestions.question: How do you set default values in destructuring?
interviewQuestions.answer: With = after the variable: const [a = 10] = [] gives a=10. const { name = 'Guest' } = {} gives name='Guest'. Defaults apply only when the value is undefined.

interviewQuestions.question: How do you rename a property in object destructuring?
interviewQuestions.answer: Use colon: const { name: userName, age: userAge } = person. This extracts person.name into userName and person.age into userAge.

interviewQuestions.question: How do you swap two variables using destructuring?
interviewQuestions.answer: [a, b] = [b, a]. This swaps the values of a and b without needing a temporary variable.

interviewQuestions.question: How does destructuring work in function parameters?
interviewQuestions.answer: function display({ name, age }) { console.log(name, age); }. Called as display({ name: 'Alice', age: 30 }). The object is destructured in the parameter list.

interviewQuestions.question: What is nested destructuring?
interviewQuestions.answer: Destructuring nested structures: const { address: { city, zip } } = person. This extracts person.address.city and person.address.zip into city and zip variables.

interviewQuestions.question: Can you mix array and object destructuring?
interviewQuestions.answer: Yes: const { users: [first, ...rest] } = data. This extracts data.users, then destructures the array into first and rest.

interviewQuestions.question: What happens if the destructured value is null or undefined?
interviewQuestions.answer: Destructuring null or undefined throws a TypeError. Always provide defaults or guard against null/undefined when destructuring potentially null values.

interviewQuestions.question: How is destructuring used in React?
interviewQuestions.answer: const [count, setCount] = useState(0) — array destructuring for state hook. const { data, loading, error } = useQuery(...) — object destructuring for query results.

codeExamples.title: Array Destructuring Basics
codeExamples.description: Array destructuring unpacks by position. Supports skipping with commas, defaults with =, rest with ..., and variable swapping.

codeExamples.title: Object Destructuring Basics
codeExamples.description: Object destructuring unpacks by property name. Supports renaming with :, defaults with =, nested destructuring, and rest with ...

codeExamples.title: Destructuring in Function Parameters
codeExamples.description: Parameter destructuring makes function signatures self-documenting. Default values protect against undefined options. The = {} default ensures destructuring works when no argument is passed.

codeExamples.title: Destructuring API Responses
codeExamples.description: Nested destructuring cleanly extracts deeply nested data from API responses. Renaming clarifies variable names from API field names.

codeExamples.title: Practical Destructuring Patterns
codeExamples.description: Everyday patterns: swapping, multiple return values, Map iteration, string extraction, and computed property destructuring.

mcqQuestions.question: What does const [a, b] = [1, 2, 3] assign?
mcqQuestions.explanation: Array destructuring assigns by position. a gets the first element (1), b gets the second (2). The third element is ignored.

mcqQuestions.question: What is the output of: const { x, y } = { x: 10, y: 20, z: 30 }; console.log(x, y);
mcqQuestions.explanation: Object destructuring extracts by property name. x gets 10, y gets 20. z is ignored.

mcqQuestions.question: How do you set a default value in destructuring?
mcqQuestions.explanation: Use = after the variable name to set a default: const { name = 'Guest' } = obj. The default applies only if the value is undefined.

mcqQuestions.question: How do you rename a destructured property?
mcqQuestions.explanation: Use colon: const { name: userName } = obj extracts obj.name into the variable userName.

mcqQuestions.question: What does const [a, , c] = [1, 2, 3] assign?
mcqQuestions.explanation: The comma skips the second element (2). a gets 1, c gets 3.

mcqQuestions.question: What happens when you destructure null? const { a } = null;
mcqQuestions.explanation: Destructuring null or undefined throws a TypeError because you cannot access properties of null/undefined.

mcqQuestions.question: What is the purpose of = {} in function({ a } = {})?
options: It provides a default empty object so calling the function without args doesn't throw
mcqQuestions.explanation: The = {} provides a default parameter value. If the function is called without arguments, it uses {} instead of undefined, preventing a TypeError.

mcqQuestions.question: What does the following log? const [x, ...y] = [1]; console.log(x, y);
mcqQuestions.explanation: x gets 1 (the first element). y collects remaining elements via rest — there are none, so y is [].

mcqQuestions.question: How do you extract properties from a nested object?
mcqQuestions.explanation: Nested destructuring uses colon + braces: const { address: { city } } = obj extracts obj.address.city into city.

mcqQuestions.question: Which pattern correctly swaps a and b?
options [
mcqQuestions.explanation: Oops, let me redo: [a, b] = [b, a] correctly swaps a and b using array destructuring. Option index 0 is correct.
